title: Marshmallow root cold infusion for digestive irritation
subtitle: A mucilaginous cold-water extract for an inflamed gut
excerpt: A cold-water infusion of dried marshmallow root, steeped overnight and taken before meals. The mucilage coats and soothes an irritated digestive tract. One of the oldest demulcent preparations in the western herbal tradition.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), marshmallow entry, demulcent and emollient actions on mucous membranes. Culpeper, The English Physician (1652), marshmallow for gut inflammation. EMA HMPC monograph on Althaea officinalis radix (2016), traditional use for mild gastrointestinal inflammation.

recipe.batchNotes: Scale up to a litre jar: 40 g root in 1 L cold water. Keeps refrigerated for 24 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Set to steep last thing at night; the infusion is ready by morning. Dried marshmallow root keeps 2 years in a sealed tin away from damp.

recipeTools.notes: Any glass jar with a tight-fitting lid works.
Or a muslin-lined strainer to catch fine root particles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that forms a protective mucilaginous coating on mucous membranes, reducing friction and inflammation.
A gel-like polysaccharide found in marshmallow root that swells in water to form a soothing, slippery liquid.

content.text: Marshmallow root steeped in cold water overnight, then strained and sipped before meals. This is the original preparation for an unsettled gut, the cold-water method is essential because heat breaks down the
that makes marshmallow root useful. Hot water produces a weaker, more bitter cup. The cold infusion is thick, slightly sweet, and mild-tasting, the

action coats the lining of the oesophagus, stomach, and intestines, and has been used for mild gut inflammation, heartburn, and the raw, irritated feeling that follows an episode of vomiting or acid reflux. Maud Grieve lists it for 'inflammatory conditions of the alimentary canal'; the EMA's 2016 monograph confirms traditional use for mild gastrointestinal inflammation.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Medication timing: the mucilage in marshmallow root may slow the absorption of other medicines taken at the same time. Take marshmallow root preparations at least one hour away from any prescribed medication.

Diabetes: marshmallow root contains polysaccharides that may have mild effects on blood sugar at high doses. If you are managing blood glucose medically, monitor your levels when first using marshmallow preparations.

See your doctor for heartburn that occurs more than twice a week, pain when swallowing, unintended weight loss, vomiting blood or material that looks like coffee grounds, or any gut symptoms in a child under two.

items.prepNote: cut and sifted dried root, about 2 heaped teaspoons
cold, straight from the tap, not boiled

content.text: The ratio is approximately 1 part root to 25 parts water by weight. This is within the EMA's dose range for traditional internal use.

Place {{marshmallow-root-dried}} of dried marshmallow root in a glass jar. Pour {{water}} of cold tap water over the root. Stir briefly, then put the lid on the jar. Leave at room temperature or in the fridge overnight, 6 to 8 hours minimum, 12 hours is ideal. The water will thicken noticeably as the mucilage releases; this is exactly what you want.

Pour the infusion through a fine sieve or muslin-lined strainer. The liquid will be slightly viscous and pale straw-coloured, thicker than water, with a mild, faintly sweet taste. Drink the cup before a meal, or after a meal if the gut is already unsettled. The infusion should be at room temperature or cool; it does not need to be heated.

One cup (250 ml) before each main meal, up to three times a day for adults. The traditional course is short, three to five days for an acute flare of digestive irritation. For ongoing gut symptoms that require daily marshmallow root, the underlying cause needs medical assessment.

Make each batch fresh. A prepared infusion keeps in the fridge for up to 24 hours; beyond that the mucilage starts to break down and the liquid becomes watery. Dried marshmallow root keeps 2 years in a sealed tin away from damp.
When NOT to use this remedy

See your doctor for heartburn that occurs more than twice a week, pain when swallowing, unintended weight loss, or black or blood-streaked stools. Skip marshmallow root preparations within one hour of prescribed medications, the mucilage may slow their absorption.

attrs.heading: If the infusion seems watery
attrs.intro: Two reasons the mucilage may not have developed properly.

items.symptom: Liquid is thin and tastes bitter rather than mildly sweet
items.cause: Old root that has lost its mucilage content, or insufficient steeping time.
items.fix: Fresh dried marshmallow root should feel slightly sticky between the fingers when moistened. Steep for the full 8, 12 hours. If the root smells musty or has been open more than two years, replace the stock.

items.symptom: Infusion is very thick and gel-like, hard to drink
items.cause: Too much root or very fresh, potent root.
items.fix: This is not a problem, dilute with an additional 50, 100 ml cold water and stir. The preparation is still fully effective.
